Normaly Im on the side of Remake Culture that tends towards the originals over Remakes, however in my opinion RE4R eclipses its original in almost every way.While i liked OG RE4 i couldnt help to feel that the pacing was abysmal and overstated its welcome massively I expected the Island to be a final set-piece rather than an entire third of the Game. I played it twice and dont feel like going back to it ever again.I played this game for almost 200hrs over PC and Console.RE4R manages to keep its pacing just right, cutting certain sections of the game and adding them back into Separate Ways also fixes the problem of that side to not just run through the main games areas again.I love the new Gameplay, adding the Knife Parry was one of the best additions to be made.If I had to critique something than it would be that Salazar is an absolute downgrade over his OG both in Design and Story. Also Aim Blooming, why this is the wrong game. I can understand the thought process on 2R and 3R but here????I played so much Mercs despite me usually not being too much into the mode. (It also makes getting the Steam Achievement completion much easier if youre struggling. THX for that Capcom.) There is something to be said about the REmakes dodging core ideas of their originals identity. RE2R dropping the zapping System, RE3R the choices, RE4R makes managing Ashley basically void unless a short interaction on a grab. I know we all famously hate Escort missions but it is also a core part of the OGs identity and a feature not often done. That being said on the other hand a more enjoyable experience come out of that, its something to think about.The overall Meta progression feels great tho it asks maybe for a few too many replays especially considering its length IMO.Also the modding support for this game while dying down now is great helping with enjoying the many replays of this game.Now Capcom take this style of Game and put Jill in it but instead of Knife Parry you build on the Dodge from 3R thx.

[h1] Good But Still Overrated [/h1][u] 7/10 [/u]RE4R is an incredibly faithful Remake. It practically does nothing but improve upon the original. Better graphics, better performance, better combat. The reason I'm giving it a 7 is because I've never been a huge fan of the original RE4 to begin with. I can't stand Ashley Trashley and I'm tired of saving her 17 times per chapter. Of course, the Remake does not change that. Also, to me personally, the origianal always felt a bit dragged-out. Of course, the Remake does not change that. The spanish village, as a setting, has always been a boring place to explore and have a game take place in, in my opinion, and of course, the Remake does not change that. There are plenty of positive things I could say as well, for example I've always loved the entire concept of The Merchent. I think it was a brilliant idea back in the day, and of course, it's done just as well here in the Remake. At the end of the day, RE4R is an incredibly faithful Remake, however, faithfully Remaking a 7/10 game does not magically make it a 10/10 in my eyes, as the problems I had with the original are all still here. I can say / write the exact same review for the Seperate Ways 'Story Expansion'.

Honestly, I dont really have anything bad to say about this game. I havent played the older Resident Evil titles from the 90s and early 2000s (The oldest RE game that I played so far was RE5), so I cant compare it to the original or stand on the nostalgia side like many longtime fans. But as someone experiencing this as a modern game, I can confidently say the remake is incredibly well done.The game is extremely detailed and packed with content. Theres always something new happening: different enemy types, multiple bosses, and a lot of gameplay mechanics that keep things fresh from start to finish. The story surrounding the Las Plagas parasite is also very interesting and offers a different direction compared to previous games that focused more on viruses.You again play as Leon S. Kennedy, and the combat feels very satisfying and more improved as Leon can now knock enemies down and follow up with powerful kicks, which makes combat feel more dynamic than in some of the earlier remakes.Another thing I really liked is the variety of locations and puzzles. The game constantly takes you to new environments, and the puzzles break up the action nicely. Like it's not focused only on one place, you're constantly on the move. My personal favorite part of the game was the castle section, and that's basically only one of the few times game tends to actually slow down.Overall, the game is full of content, mechanics, atmosphere, and memorable moments.For me, its a perfect score.10/10!

I played this in April 2025, and honestly, I was awestruck by the quality. As someone who grew up with the original GameCube version, I went in with high expectationsand this remake still managed to surprise me. Its not a 1:1 recreation, but it captures the spirit, the overthetop action, and the wild adventure of Leons journey in a way that feels both nostalgic and fresh.One of the biggest highlights for me is how they handled Luis Sera. His story feels more present, more human, and honestly more relatable. It plays like a redemption arc, and it adds emotional weight the original never fully explored.The parry system is another standout. Being able to counter attacks while on the brink of danger feels incredibly satisfying and adds a whole new layer of strategy. Its one of those mechanics that makes you wonder how is not present in other RE entries before.The RE Engine continues to be a monster. The graphics, the atmosphere, the lighting, the goreeverything is dialed up. The horror moments hit harder, the environments feel alive, and the game manages to be memorable while still offering a different take on the classic.I beat both the main game and Separate Ways on Hard, and Im already planning a Professional playthrough to unlock the remaining special weapons and items. This is the kind of game that makes you want to keep pushing yourself. Also tried Mercenaries Mode is pretty fun like the OG game! I think beside more character unlocks, there is also some special weapons I can get there.Only real issue I ran into was a strange bug: if you have too many save files, the loading screen to the main menu takes way too long. So maybe keep your save list tidy to avoid that slowdown.Overall, this remake is everything I hoped for and more. Now Im just hoping we get to see RE9 soonespecially if it continues Leons development and maybe even brings us back to Raccoon City.10/10  A brilliant reimagining that stands proudly beside the original.

I love the original game, have completed it many, many times and I have to say, what Capcom did with the remake is amazing. The most important take away from the whole game is, that this is not a remaster, its a remake. In its core, its the same game but almost everything about it has been remade and plenty of things have been added to it. Its not supposed to replace the original, its just another take at Resident Evil 4The game is very different, yet the same. Bigger changes include stuff like:- Very, very expanded map that s no longer constrained by 20 year old tech. The game goes through the same main "hubs" as the original, but no area is left the same. They added plenty of addition, expansion and scale to each area, so going through them is very fresh and the game is much longer, if you don t rush through it. - A revised combat system. The whole thing has been modernized. You can now move while shooting, which feels great. They balanced it by increasing the number of enemies you face and adding more enemy variation and new enemies. The knife also plays a much bigger part. It has durability, you can stealth take down enemies with it and also parry attacks as sort of quick time event, where you have to time your block well, but that drains durability. Overall, the combat feels very fluid and very fun. Enemy hitboxes still work great and you play the game basically the same way as the original, where you try to shoot weak points to execute melee takedowns. The balancing is great, even with all these changes, the remake is definitely harder than the og. Especially on profeasional. - New & old familiar weapons, new enemy types, side objectives, some characters (like Luis) being a bigger part of the story, collectibles, crafting system, more lore, weapon wheel and moreGame also includes Mercenaries mode from the original for free and its also very fun. + it includes some new characters compared to the original. You can also buy Separate ways as a paid DLC, which is also heavily revised, even though I haven t yet played through the whole DLC, what i have played seems good too.Performance vise its fantastic. Game is very well optimized and there s very few bugs.Re4 remake is a fantastic game & it can easily face off against the mainline RE games. Very much recommended, weather or not you have played the OG or not. If you have, the remake still feels extremely fresh and if you haven t you re in for a great experience. Easily worth the full price.

This review is for the base game only and doesn't factor in the Separate Ways DLC.This is the best Resident Evil game by a mile. The graphics are beautiful and everything is super realistic, it's a huge upgrade from the original. Compared to the original, the story is significantly better because it explains everything in more detail. You always understand what's going on and why things happen, while in the original there are a lot of unanswered questions. However, when we just look at the story itself and not compare it to anything, it's still really good. The combat is super fun and satisfying. The game adds a "parry system", which wasn't present in the original. The parry system really does change the combat a ton and adds a lot more of a reward in the combat. I think this game perfected the third person shooter. Also, the game is super good at rewarding you. When finishing certain challenges, such as beating the game on S+ on Professional difficulty, you unlock accessories which give you perks that make you more overpowered. This makes finishing challenges actually worth it and definitely make it worth the while.When you start going for platinum, the game definitely gets a bit frustrating. Enemies have a ton of HP and don't stagger nearly as much, meaning you're left more vulnerable. Also, the game loves to stun-lock you (stun locking is whenever the game temporarily prevents you from moving) a lot, which means sometimes you will take a ton of damage and it won't even be your fault. On lower difficulties, most enemies can be killed with a simple headshot + kick, but on higher difficulties, it takes a lot more to kill them. I definitely don't recommend the casual player play on harder difficulties, because it isn't the easiest. (SPOILER ALERT) One thing I really didn't like about this game is how is they started throwing out regeneradors before you get the biosensor scope. I took a break from this game for a few days when I got to this section on my first playthrough because I couldn't get past it. When I was going for the Minimalist achievement (handgun and knife only run,) chapter 13 was the bane of my existence because I couldn't kill the regeneradors. After finally finishing the platinum for this game, I can definitely say this is my favorite Resident Evil, and the pros heavily outweigh the cons. It only starts to get a little frustrating when you go for platinum.

Despite being different from the previous entries, RE4 is honestly incredible. The only criticism I have is that performance could be better in a few chapters, even after optimizing the settings, but that's about it. RE 4 Remake is masterful. The game constantly puts your aim to the test, throwing you into intense situations where precision and quick reactions matter. Resident Evil 4 Remake  The Best Action Shooter in the Resident Evil SeriesIn my opinion, Resident Evil 4 Remake is the best action-focused game in the Resident Evil series. It perfectly blends intense combat with the classic survival horror atmosphere the franchise is known for.The gameplay feels incredibly satisfying. The enemies are aggressive, encounters stay tense, and the combat constantly keeps you engaged. Even though the game leans more toward action, it still keeps the survival aspect alive by making you manage your resources and think carefully about how you approach each fight.One of my favorite parts of the game is the large amount of collectibles and upgrades. Exploring every corner of the map feels rewarding because there is almost always something useful to find. This makes exploration feel meaningful instead of just optional.The weapon upgrade system is also fantastic. You can upgrade your weapons through the Merchant and customize them to match your playstyle. Being able to equip attachments like laser sights or scopes adds another layer of depth and makes each weapon feel more personal and satisfying to use.Another thing that really stands out is the incredible graphical detail. The game looks absolutely stunning. The environments are atmospheric and beautifully crafted, but what impressed me the most is the level of detail on the characters themselves. You can actually see the woven fabric in the characters clothing, the texture of leather gear, and the small details in their outfits. It makes the characters feel much more realistic and grounded.Lighting, shadows, and environmental effects also contribute a lot to the atmosphere. Whether you're walking through the foggy village, exploring the castle, or fighting through darker areas, the visuals constantly help build tension and immersion.After finishing both the main story and the Separate Ways DLC, it was also really fun to jump into Mercenaries Mode. Its a great way to wrap up the experience and just enjoy the combat mechanics without worrying about the story. The mode is fast-paced, score-based, and perfect if you want to push your skills and try different characters and strategies.Overall, Resident Evil 4 Remake is an outstanding experience with a great mix of action, exploration, weapon customization, incredible graphics, and classic Resident Evil tension.MUST PLAY for anyone who enjoys action-heavy Resident Evil games.

When I think of remakes, Capcom immediately comes to mind. Especially the Resident Evil series. Resident Evil 4 (2023) proves that remakes (or remasters) can absolutely be done right. When handled properly, they give players a great reason to return to a classic and relive the experience of the original game while still feeling fresh and modern.The outstanding graphics, excellent sound design, and impressive optimization make buying this game feel completely worthwhile. It doesnt leave you disappointed or feeling like youve wasted your money. The atmosphere is darker and more immersive than ever, and the updated gameplay makes combat feel smoother and more intense.Playing as Leon S. Kennedy still feels just as exciting as it did in the original, but the remake adds better mechanics, improved enemy behavior, and more polished level design. Even familiar moments like escorting Ashley Graham through dangerous areas feel more natural thanks to the refined AI and gameplay adjustments. At the same time, the remake stays faithful to what made the original special, keeping iconic locations, tense encounters, and the unique atmosphere that fans remember.Overall, Resident Evil 4 (2023) shows exactly how a remake should be done: respecting the original while improving nearly every aspect of it. It feels both nostalgic and new at the same time, which is exactly what fans hope for when revisiting a classic.

Resident Evil 4 in PC VR (I modded this to have praydog's VR) is an absolute blast and easily one of the most fun VR shooters Ive played. The mix of horror, action, and smooth gunplay feels incredible when youre physically aiming, reloading, and fighting off waves of enemies up close. Every encounter feels intense and hands-on, especially when youre surrounded.Although I didn't get to play it with motion sensor controllers, I played it with the PS5 controller and VR headset, the immersion makes classic moments hit way hardvillage fights, cabin defenses, and boss battles feel chaotic and cinematic in the best way. The weapons feel great to use, the pacing never slows down, and its insanely satisfying both to play and replay.It wasn't very scary as other RE games i have experienced in my opinion, but it makes up for it with pure fun, variety, and nonstop action. It is one of the longer Resident Evil games out there.A great VR experience. I can see why this game is among one of Resident Evil fan players' favorite. Consequently, it is arguably fighting Resident 2 being in my top 3. Very action based so if you're into nonstop action, this game is it. I probably prefer more horror in it. However, I still highly recommend it.

objectively speaking a good game. hated everything about its action focus and slant, however, and it's my most honest belief that resident evil is at its best when doing slower, more cerebral survival horror action than the high octane "gears of evil" nonsense that is this game.if you liked re4 in its original state, you'll like this remake, because it's the same game, only more polished and actually atmospheric and playable. if you liked re4 in its original state or this game, you're not allowed to complain about the direction the series took with re5 because that game is only a logical conclusion of the process re4 started and everyone loved.while re4r is a very well polished and developed game, the action slant of its gameplay, in my opinion, doesn't actually work very well with resident evil's overall design in the way its characters move and what they can do in the world they inhabit. it just became obnoxious after a while. mercenaries mode is competent high octane action until it isn't and you straight up lack the tools to handle what it throws your way (which in a way is intentional, since the game ramps up in difficulty until you cannot go any further because that's how its arcade design works, but the nonsense mercenaries pulls isn't entirely different from the nonsense the main game pulls). there's multiple moments in the game where you'll be hit or killed and it's not going to feel like "oh, i should have acted differently or done things better in this specific way", it just feels obnoxious.the series very seriously needed its re7 reboot and i'm glad the series went back to the horror it's actually good at. but given re4make's success and how half of re9 is more of the action nonsense, we may be headed down a bad route yet again. only time will tell what happens next.

[h1]You're a long way from home, Cowboy..[/h1]This Resident Evil game is one of the best ones in the franchise. This remake was very much needed for the Resident Evil franchise. I'll start off by saying that the OG Resident Evil 4 from 2005 does top this remake but this remake was generally really impressing.The story is as always good in this game. The starting area of the game was really good introducing us to the village and the villagers who has hostile ideas. The objective is simple, go through the village, find the President's Daughter *Baby Eagle* and then get the hell outta there. But, as always, it isn't THAT simple in a Resident Evil game.The characters in this game are really good, with Ramon Salazar, Krauser, Osmund Saddler and Ashley Graham. They all contain specific personalities which makes them feel completely different from each other. Osmund Saddler is seen to be that mysterious Antagonist from the early hours of the game to the cocky and more over-confident during the last hours of the game. Ramon Salazar is one of the bosses with the most cocky personality throughout the game, making him a nuisance in the way of Leon. Krauser was good as a antagonist but it was sad to see the ending. The combat in this game was pretty cool, the gun mechanics were awesome and the melee combat with Leon and Ada were just amazing and pretty badass.The setting and the environment in this game were one of my favorites as it is set in a village full of religious cults in Rural Spain. The areas and the stages throughout the game was refreshing and pretty neat which hella tons of detail in each singular level. The Soundtrack of this game is just amazing, one of the best soundtracks ever made in my opinion.The DLC Separate Ways of Resident Evil 4 is also of the best DLC's. It was really fun to play and the story was good.The cinematics in this game and the graphics are some of the best graphics and cutscenes I have ever seen in any video game. They are made with much detail and the cutscenes feels realistic to watch.It was a pain to 100% complete this game because of the achievements which I have to unlock by completing the game on the hardest difficulty, I was almost going to rage quit but I did fight through and got 100% achievement.Really enjoyed this game on my 1st playthrough and was really tough during the final hours.Overall Rating -  (10/10)

I'll be upfront. I was never the biggest fan of the original RE4. The tank controls in RE games have never clicked with me. That's entirely on me, since I never play these games with a controller, which is clearly how they were designed to be played. That said, starting with the RE2 remake onward, controlling characters with mouse and keyboard has become significantly less of a chore compared to when tank controls were the standard.Now that I can actually get into this game and the two previous remakes, I can confidently say this is one of the best games I've ever played. The level of detail in the RE engine, combined with the gameplay tweaks, improved controls, and enhanced exploration, has made this game a fantastic addition to my library. Despite being roughly a 20-hour experience, I never found the game dragging at all. I've even replayed it on professional difficulty for those sweet achievement hunting opportunities.Solid game. Ashley is less annoying but still gets on my nerves. She's constantly up Leon's ass and whining about one thing or another. Not a dealbreaker though. I'm obviously just being a cranky old man about this.10/10 Buy this game and play it.

Resident Evil 4 is an incredible reimagining of one of the most influential action-horror games ever made. It takes everything fans loved about the original and enhances it with modern visuals, improved gameplay, and a darker, more immersive atmosphere.Playing as Leon S. Kennedy, youre sent on a mission to rescue the presidents daughter, Ashley Graham, from a mysterious cult in rural Europe. The story is engaging from start to finish, with great pacing and plenty of memorable encounters that keep you hooked the entire time.The combat feels fantastic. Every weapon has weight and impact, and the improved movement and parry mechanics make encounters with enemies feel more intense and strategic. Facing off against the terrifying villagers infected by Las Plagas never gets old, especially when the action ramps up during the games larger battles and boss fights.Visually, the game is stunning. The environmentsfrom eerie villages to massive castlesare beautifully detailed and packed with atmosphere. The sound design and music also do an amazing job of building tension, making every new area feel unpredictable and dangerous.What really stands out is how well the remake balances horror and action. It keeps the intense, cinematic moments the original was known for while adding deeper gameplay and a more grounded tone. Longtime fans will appreciate how faithfully it honors the original, while new players get a polished, modern survival horror experience.Overall, Resident Evil 4 is a masterpiece of a remake and easily one of the best entries in the series. Its thrilling, atmospheric, and endlessly fun to playan absolute must for fans of action, horror, or great game design.

[h1] 9/10 [/h1]Among the best entries in Resident Evil, the remakes have always stood out for better and for worse. They allow newcomers to access a wonderful franchise without having to power through old, janky mechanics that don't really fly in this day and age anymore. With the success of Resident Evil 2 Remake (and the not so success of Resident Evil 3 Remake) Resident Evil 4 was inevitably going to be remade as well. After playing the remake twice now without even touching the OG, it's hard for me not to say that this is the ultimate way to experience the game. Does that make Resident Evil 4 (2005) a bad game? Not at all. But considering that this is the highest rated Resident Evil, you just want to call this superior. Everything in this game has been scientifically designed to be as addictive as humanly possible, from something as simple as treasure hunting by frequently checking the map like a crack addict to the horrible shooting range that makes me wanna die (seriously whoever designed 2-A has gotta go.) I was unfortunately subject to a lot of graphical screen-tearing throughout my playthrough, alongside an abundance of crashes, screen flickering like crazy when I opened the game, and having to manually switch the game over to my primary monitor when it would randomly open on my secondary monitor for no reason. Also with my specs I was quite surprised at how demanding it was for my computer, I rarely have to turn down games graphically to help stabilize FPS or other graphical anomalies, but these problems are nothing issues due to the game being overall fantastic. Speaking briefly about Separate Ways, it honestly enhanced my love for the game even more. Being able to watch Leon's adventure through Ada's eyes while dealing with your own adventure as her made me nerd out a couple times, it was very cool to learn Ada was the one who rung the bell to save Leon from the initial village siege. Cool stuff like that happened the entire DLC, these things are one of the many reasons I love Outlast: Whistleblower.

After the removal of Denuvo & Enigma DRM I can recommend this game. There was no need for DRM that can cripple others hardware, yes I know it won't affect me much since I have a pretty beefy rig but it ruins entry level hardware.Aside from that, this game is radically different front the original RE4. I recommend playing RE4(2005) with the HD project mod then play this. Both are great in their own way but 2005 RE4 should be the first time go for people new to RE4 unless for some reason the tank controls bother you too much.

I already own and played the game twice on PlayStation, once in 2024 right after finishing the OG RE4, and the second time literally just a month ago or so, and I literally only got it on Steam as well now so I could play it with a mod that replaces Leon with Luis. I love this man so much, guys. The game lacks some lines I miss from the original, and I kind of preferred RE4 OG Leon, but I will admit that remake Leon is definitely more realistic, just a bit less silly, and that I low-key prefer the RE4 remake over the original (though that might just be mainly because of Luis). The biggest upgrade of this remake, however, was Luis (imo). He was such a small character in the original, pretty much immediately got impaled by Saddlers massive shlong, and was kind of weird towards Ashley, which I didnt really like, but Id say he wouldve had potential in the OG as well, and the remake really showed his potential as a character (weird wording but I hope it makes sense). Luis is such a great character, and Im so happy Capcom made him so amazing in the remake, maybe Im glazing him too much, I literally have a photocard of him and all. Oh my god, I love him so much. Buying this game on Steam just for the mod mightve been a dumb decision, but god forbid a girl yearns for more Luis content. Shoutout to Luis Serra. Ashley is also like WAYYY better in the remake, especially her AI, which makes sense considering the OG is literally 22 years old by now. Obviously the OG Ashleys AI wasnt as good, but also her voice lines arent as annoying anymore, and I prefer Leons and Ashleys relationship way more in the remake than in the original. To be honest this whole review is just an excuse for me to geek out over Luis, and Im glad I got it on steam so I could write this review. 10/10. #bringbackluisserra2026. Count your days Capcom. Truly a masterpiece remade with love to honor the original, and the absolute King of the genre.I admit I am biased as the original RE4 is one of my favorite games of all time. For me it was the game I'd sit and replay over and over simply to relive some of it's best moments or simply enjoy the combat. If the younger me playing the original on Gamecube version were given this instead; it probably would have ruined my life. Everything I remember about my many playthroughs is alive and well in the remake.This iteration of RE4 is absolutely the benchmark all game developers should attempt to measure up to when remaking beloved classics. Nothing was stripped away that made the original special and the additions only expand the gameplay and story to be greater that it was before.If you like the idea of a corny badass cop going to a remote village in the woods to find a missing girl only to be swept up in way way more than he was bargaining for; you'll love RE4. If you could care less about story and just want a feast of combat encounters; you'll love RE4. If you want a solid world, pacing, memorable characters and boss-fights; you will love RE4.

Resident Evil 4 is easily one of the most polished entries in the Resident Evil series.The graphics are outstanding and clearly a step above the earlier remakes like Resident Evil 2 and Resident Evil 3. The environments are detailed, atmospheric, and varied throughout the game.Compared to RE2, this game leans much more into action. There is a lot more gunplay and larger combat encounters, but it still keeps the tension and pacing engaging. The boss battles were one of the highlights for me. They are creative, memorable, and feel very different from each other.The Separate Ways DLC is also worth mentioning. It runs alongside the main campaign and shows the story from Ada Wongs perspective. It doesnt feel like a recycled version of Leons story either. Ada has different mechanics and tools that keep the gameplay fresh, and her storyline adds interesting context to the main events.One of the few frustrations I had was the inventory management. Unlike earlier Resident Evil games, you cant freely store items like extra herbs or ammo. This sometimes forced me to carry items I wasnt planning to use or discard them entirely, which felt a bit limiting.Overall, the game is long, highly polished, and has great replay value. Between the main campaign, the DLC, and the different weapons and upgrades, theres plenty of reason to come back for another run.Definitely worth the money.

Played through this in 2026. I can't believe I missed this game when it first came out. What a brilliant game. The game has a story that takes you to unexpected places with a satisfying conclusion. It is challenging but not unfair and rewards skill. Loadout decisions you make matter, and the game systems are polished and balanced. It made me realize how much this game must have inspired other games like Half Life Alyx or The Last of Us Part 1. The game just grips you into its world and you will find pure escapism. That said, creepy and terrifying escapism. The game's in-game vendor cuts through the tension with some whimsy and humor sort of like a splash of yoghurt on some good chili. It is crazy to see how far behind this level of quality modern gaming has become. I had almost forgotten what a good game feels like. Also, playing this in 2026 with all the graphics cranked up to maximum and 200+fps is an absolute treat. The engine does a great job. No stutters or hitching in sight.

I came to Resident Evil 4 Remake a little late, but wow... this game absolutely deserves the praise it gets.The first thing that hit me was how incredible it looks. The environments, lighting, and detail in the world are fantastic. The village especially feels alive in a really unsettling way. When the villagers start hunting you, it genuinely feels chaotic and dangerous  not just enemies spawning in, but an angry mob that wants you gone.What really surprised me though is how different the horror feels compared to the usual Resident Evil formula. Instead of the classic corporate bio-weapon outbreak, this story leans into something darker and more mysterious. The cult atmosphere, the strange rural setting, and the constant pressure from enemies surrounding you give the game its own identity within the series.I originally stopped playing after getting stuck on a boss fight and drifted away from it. But after finishing Resident Evil 9: Requiem, my motivation to see more of the Resident Evil story came back in full force.Coming back to it now, Im reminded why people love this game so much. Its tense, beautiful, and still manages to feel fresh even years after the original.Definitely worth experiencing.

TL:DR rating - 8/10I remember playing this game almost 20 years ago on a pc with integrated graphics at like 25fps and thinking the graphics were amazing and an action zombie game could not be any cooler.Fast forward to current day, the visuals look absolutely incredible like all of the modern RE games and remakes.To be clear, this is not a "horror" game per se. For almost all of the game, its more of an action focused zombie shooter with inventory management. And its a fabulous one at that.There is some "horror" gameplay however when you play as Ashley who can't blow away zombie heads like Leon can.For the first 4-5 hours, I was missing the horror inspired atmosphere from the RE2 remake(yes it still has Leon blowing away zombies but he isn't quite as badass there in the beginning as he is in RE4).Once I stopped comparing the RE4 remake to RE2 and looked at it like the hybrid horror action shooter game it is, things became a lot more enjoyable.The quality of the remake is fantastic and is a super fun time. Audio, Visuals, Map design all are top notch and the polish is fantastic.

Boy oh boy, where do I even begin. Leon... honey, my pookie bear. I have loved you ever since I first laid eyes on you in Resident Evil 2. The way you walk into Raccoon City with nothing but courage, striking fear into the hearts of every zombie and monster that dares cross your path. Your calm aim, that smooth one-liner confidence, and that iconic jacket... absolutely gorgeous. I would do anything for you. I wish it were possible to freeze time so I would never have to see you grow older through every mission and nightmare. You had a rough startyour first day as a cop turning into a full-blown apocalypsebut you never gave up hope. You fought through it all, protecting people like Claire Redfield and Sherry Birkin, even when the whole world was falling apart around you. Youre amazing off the battlefield too. Loyal, brave, and always trying to do the right thingeven when the government throws you into impossible missions. Sometimes I even call you dad. I forever dread and weep thinking about the day you might finally hang up the handgun and retire from saving the world. I would sacrifice my own life if it were the only thing that could put a smile on your beautiful face. Youve given me so much joy and heartbreak over the years. I remember when I first saw you struggle and survive the horrors of Raccoon City. It felt like my heart shattered watching everything fall apart. But deep down, I knew my glorious king would survive. Then you kept coming back stronger. Saving the presidents daughter Ashley Graham in Resident Evil 4 like the absolute legend you are. Facing impossible odds again and again. And when you showed up years later, older but even cooler in Resident Evil 6, I realized something... my sweet baby boy never stopped fighting. You may be getting older now, but youre still the GOAT of survival horror, my GOAT. You didnt just survive the outbreakyou became the legend everyone looks up to. I love you, pookie bear. My glorious king, Leon S. Kennedy.

OG RE4 is my 3rd favorite game of all time. I had alot of trepidation when Capcom announced this remake. Would they  it up? How can you outdo the perfection that is OG RE4? It had (and still does) a certain charm and camp to it that would be almost impossible to replicate. All of that went out the window after about 5 minutes into this game. Much like the Dead Space 2023 remake and the Final Fantasy 7 remakes, this game is absolute perfection. While I still would recommend playing OG RE4 (I still go back and replay OG RE4), absolutely EVERYTHING about this game is perfect. Graphics, atmosphere, music, voice acting, sound effects, visuals, gameplay, everything. My SINGLE complaint is Ada's voice actress; they shoulda used the actress from REmake 2. In this one, I genuinely think REmake 4's Lily Gao fine, great even, it's just REmake 2's Jolene Andersen was absolutely perfect for Ada. That is such a miniscule thing for me though, because this game is just absolute perfection. Also quick shoutout to Nick Apostolides. He was great in REmake 2, but he  KILLED it in this. Perfectly translated Paul Mercier's voice delivery from OG RE4 into this darker, more brutal toned remake. I love this game so much. An absolute masterpiece.

Excellent, grounded reimagining of RE4. Grounded as-in character development / expression; not sure how real a RE game can feel. Gameplay-wise, the core loop is a great mix of positioning, resource pressure, and aggressive enemies.The guns are awesome. Shots feel punchy, reloads have great sound and animation, and the pistol holding an extra round in the chamber is a satisfying detail. Also: using a scope can be dangerous in close quarters and boss fights, but it can also swing encounters. Really wish adaptive triggers from the PS5 made their way here for DuelSense controllers.The Merchant upgrade economy creates real, interesting tradeoffs, and trying to upgrade everything / trying out late game weapons are why youll probably want to replay. Damage has clear impact, reload speed gets you out of tight binds, ammo capacity avoids reloading, rate of fire becomes more necessary late game, and increasing knife durability is essential for protecting yourself. Knife parries are satisfying, but I didnt use them enough on my first run. Perfect parries are difficult, but they open enemies up for a devastating counter that always feel awesome.Horror is different in this to other titles in the series, but I believe its still effective while youre learning how to control Leon. Like most REs, fear fades later. There is one exception latter in the game.Early on, enemies create uniquely tense encounters while you are still learning controls and limited to knife + pistol.The least scary sections are when you are clearly entering a shooting gallery space. These spaces feel less like a lived-in environment and more like a clear set-piece. Theres also a heavy reliance on having environments filled with a bunch of wooden planks and fences that dont feel like theyd be organic to the village. Inventory is improved via auto-sort, but it can be a bit stupid.If you technically have room for something from the merchant or in gameplay, the inventory will not always sort itself to make that space. You still have to manually re-arrange to create the exact space you need.Story-wise, its a solid, character-focused narrative with a strong emotional through-line for Leon.Luis arc hits hardest through documents, and his background adds real depth. Bottom line: a near-ideal modern RE action-horror game. Best-in-class combat feel and upgrade economy. Horror peaks early but still has standout late-game spikes. Story is straightforward but character work (especially Luis and Leon) carry it hard. Stellar vocal performances and character animation.

There are games you enjoy, and then there are games that remind you why you love the medium in the first place. RE4 is the latter. It's comfortably in my top 5 games of all time.The opening village sequence is iconic for a reason. You're one person, they just keep coming, and by the time it's over you realize this game is something else entirely. Pure chaos. One of the best opening hours in gaming.Everything that made the previous entries great is here, and then some. Leon is back, trained, confident, and at his absolute peak, and that shift in character energy sets the tone for the whole experience. This isn't survival horror in the traditional sense. It's a relentlessly entertaining action game that still knows when to make your skin crawl. The balance is remarkable.What sets it apart is how much fun it is. The castle alone could be its own game. Ashley is a better character than she had any right to be. The enemy variety keeps you on your toes from start to finish, the puzzles actually make you think, and "What're ya buying" will live in your head forever. Each chapter throws something new at you and somehow it all lands.The DLC is not an afterthought either. It reframes the main story in a meaningful way and ties directly into the ending, making the full package feel even more complete.And when you step back and look at the full picture, the story, the characters, the combat, the weapons, the treasure hunting, the side quests, the minigames, the shop, the DLC, Mercenaries mode, the scope of this game compared to the previous remakes is just insane. It has everything. Coming in as a new RE fan I had no idea what I was walking into. Now I do.

This is a pretty solid remake of the classic Resident Evil 4, and does just about everything right. It stays largely true to the source material in terms of narrative and gameplay feel, brings forward many of the mini-games or side quests from the original, and reintroduces many familiar and iconic set pieces, but it reimagines a lot of things too. The graphics and sound design have been seriously upgraded, the environments, even familiar ones that carry over from the original, have been re-imagined to have a more believable/realistic layout and design, and there's several events that are expanded upon with additional or extended/reworked cutscenes or documents that add to the lore. The voice acting is less cheesy too. There is some retconning of events from the original 2005 version that takes place, but none of it was so egregious that it bothered me, and in fact I think I now prefer this version of the game. The gun range is there, though re-worked to behave slightly differently; the merchant, the combine-able items that can be sold for upgraded gear, etc. are all still present. Keep in mind though that with the upgraded and expanded world map, items will not be where you remember them. Some items that were previously only available late-game thru the merchant may now be hidden in optional areas within the game world, so be sure to explore everything thoroughly as you play. They've even re-worked Ashley so she's a little less annoying. If you forget her standing at the top of a ledge, she won't just stand there and scream for you to come catch her indefinitely. You "can" still help catch her, but if you'd rather not, or just forget, she will eventually jump or climb down on her own, which makes her slightly less helpless and annoying.As far as performance goes, it ran great on my main PC and Steam Deck both. On my desktop I was able to crank everything to max at 1080p and hold my 120fps cap.If I could levy one complaint against this version of the game, it's that they've continued with this modern gaming trend of painting the "correct" way forward in yellow paint which, maybe I'm just weird, feels insulting to our intelligence and softens the feeling of satisfaction that comes from discovering things on your own. There's so much cool environmental storytelling that goes on, and it's so nice to revisit an old favorite with a new coat of paint, but if I'm in an area for the first time and there's a clearly broken railing, it's pretty obvious I can or am supposed to go thru there without having it be the only item in the entire area that's randomly covered in splatters of yellow paint.

You couldn't ask for a better review for this remake. There's no denial that Resident Evil 4 (2002) was a masterpiece, and best Resident Evil game of all time. It was my very first one, and one that I was able to overcome my fear of horror games, and also played numerous times out of pure enjoyment.It was a lot of packed with actions too. This remake was just an upgrade. For everything that you played from 2002.The gameplay for quick time event in regards to quality of life has improved significantly, and also a lot of unnecessary QTE happened in 2002's RE4 has been removed for the better convenience in this remake. The gameplay for Ashley has improved where you accidentally killed her numerous times with poor NPC reaction and control has been removed for 2002, and the fear factor while playing her in this remake has significantly improved to a point that it makes you feel a lot scarier to play as Ashley in this remake comparing to the other one.The parrying system in this remake with a knife is nothing but a chef's kiss, and also gun handling has improved with a dodge technique they implemented from RE3 Remake. (Only if RE3 Remake was this good.)Ada's Gameplay is significantly fun to play too, and Mercenaries are fun to play. While I am not a big fan of Mercenaries, this is fun for even casuals.Remake releases and new Resident Evil launches has never ceased to amaze me, and I look forward of Code Veronica, 0, 1, and 5 in the distant future. Capcom is just on a roll.

Resident Evil 4 is easily one of the best games Ive played in recent years. Its packed with content, the gameplay is incredibly smooth, and the sound design and graphics are top-tier. One thing that doesnt get talked about enough is the sheer number of enemies the game throws at you. Youre constantly kept on your toes as new threats appear from all directions, which keeps the action intense and refreshing throughout the entire experience. On top of that, the game offers a massive arsenal of weapons to experiment with, allowing you to tailor your playstyle however you like.Getting this game for 7.99 honestly feels like daylight robbery, it almost feels like I should have paid more for the amount of content and quality you get.My initial thoughts were a bit mixed, though. I had just finished playing through Resident Evil 1, Resident Evil 0, Resident Evil 2 Remake, and Resident Evil 3 Remake, so the shift in direction was immediately noticeable. Resident Evil 4 takes a very different approach. The action is turned way up, youre given far more combat options, and you have access to a large portable inventory. Enemies also tend to go down fairly easily for the most part.Because of this, the survival horror elements feel toned down compared to earlier entries in the series. Playing on Standard difficulty, I never found myself running out of ammo or healing items, and I wasnt particularly scared like I was in previous games. Since the game makes you feel so capable and well-equipped, theres a sense of security that naturally reduces the tension.At first, I wasnt sure how I felt about this shift. Its a complete 180 compared to the earlier titles. However, once the gameplay clicks and you get into the flow of combat, it becomes incredibly addictive. The pacing, encounters, and mechanics all come together in a way that just works.As soon as I finished the main campaign, I immediately picked up the DLC, which was just as fun, if not even more enjoyable in some ways. The story was engaging and the pacing felt faster and more focused. It genuinely felt like a second playthrough from a new perspective, similar to playing Claires campaign in Resident Evil 2 Remake. Seeing the events of the story from Adas side added a lot more depth and helped build her character further.Overall, this was an incredibly fun experience. Just be prepared for a major gameplay shift if youre coming straight from the earlier Resident Evil titles.Score: 8.7 / 10

So after playing Resident Evil 2 Remake, I jumped into Resident Evil 4 Remake and honestly this game is on another level. It keeps the horror vibe but adds way more action, and it feels really polished.First thing I noticed was the graphics. The environments look amazing. The village, the castle, the forests everything looks super detailed and dark in a good way. It really feels like youre in this creepy isolated place.Gameplay is where the game really shines. The combat feels smooth and satisfying. Shooting enemies, parrying with the knife, upgrading weapons everything feels really well designed. The enemies are aggressive too, so fights can get pretty intense.Leon is also way cooler in this remake. His character feels more serious compared to the older version, but he still has those funny one liners sometimes which makes it fun.One thing I really liked is the merchant system. Finding treasure, upgrading weapons, and choosing what to buy actually makes you think about your playstyle. And hearing Whatre ya buyin? never gets old.The boss fights are also great. They feel bigger and more cinematic than before, and each one feels different which keeps the game exciting.

A masterpiece, but RE2 still holds my crown.Resident Evil 4 is just straight-up good. Like, very, very good. Ive knocked out RE1, RE2, and RE7, and I can confidently say RE4 takes the solid #2 spot on my list, right behind RE2.At first, the game felt incredibly long, but I'll admit that's mostly on me for overthinking and second-guessing every single puzzle. Looking back, the length is actually a huge plus. RE2 is my favorite in the franchise, but it felt like it ended a little too quickly. RE4 gives you way more time to actually play.Everything else is top-tier. The writing is amazing, the game is incredibly well-paced, the characters and voice acting are great, and the graphics are amazing. Plus, it ran flawlessly for me. Zero bugs the entire playthrough.And, of course, you get to see way more of Ada Wong in this one, which is an automatic win. Next stop: Resident Evil 3.

To be honest, I wasn't the biggest fan of this one. I knew going in that it would be different from other entries, but it truly felt like a pure action game with zero horror. Even Village, which follows a similar blueprint, managed to actually scare me in several parts. For a game focused so much on 'action,' many elements felt frustratingly slowthe movement, the reload animations, and the recovery time after being hit often led to cheap deaths where youd get stunned and then swamped by enemies out of nowhere.The death mechanics could be incredibly stressful, especially with those one-shot kills that felt unearned or QTEs that didn't seem to trigger properly. And then there's the tone... it's incredibly cheesy. Leons constant one-liners and over-the-top acrobatics are definitely part of his 'charm' and I really do like it, but here it was almost too much to take seriously for me, even for a zombie game. (Then again, I suppose it's the whole charm of the game and that's not bad per se).Surprisingly, Ashley was the character I liked the most throughout the journey. Playing as Ada was cool too, although I have to admit her new voice took me completely out of the experiencenothing against the VA, but the change was just too jarring. [b]Its not a bad game by any means,[/b] but Ill always prefer the vibe of Resident Evil 2. Also, shoutout to Luis; he was easily the MVP.

I started this franchise with the second game, which was great. But when I hopped over to this one, it felt like a downgrade. Now, the game is good. It's good enough for me to recommend you give it a shot. But never play it as your first game. The graphics are good, and the character designs are amazing. This game had less annoying puzzles, and it felt much simpler. However, sometimes there's a lack of horror in a survival horror game. The only scary stuff in this game was the verdugo, regenerators, and the villagers with those flying flesh heads. Some dialogue felt bland though, like how when you fought that one soldier guy he just spams the word rookie and talks about how he's much better, and how you're undertrained for the entire fight. Also, Luis is overglazed. The merchant is the goat, he has everything, and he really helps you throughout the game, and I think the concept of him was smart. Some parts tend to feel a little repetitive, shoot villager, shotgun flying flesh head, roundhouse kick and a corny one-liner. There isn't much to go on about in this game, but there is a lot of action over horror. Overall, I recommend it. It was good and fun to play, and further helps you on Leon's story for the people who are just getting into the franchise for Requiem. Score: 8.3/10

Now that there are no additional DRM layers, I can happily recommend this.DRM past the first couple weeks of release is literally useless; at that point the game is already cracked so the only effect of DRM at that point is to make the paid version of the game objectively worse than pirating the game. As for the game itself:If you are a fan of Resident Evil 4:Wait for a sale. The visuals are nice, but they spent a lot of time changing things that didnt need changing and in areas where they didnt change much, the updated combat makes those sections incredibly easy even with the increased numbers of enemies.The entire final act of the game has been shifted around in a way that makes it feel lile they ran out of time or ideas.If you aren't already an RE4 fan:Yeah just buy it it's great.If they add more DRM maybe don't bother buying, but for now it's definitely worth it.Ignore the 2 hour play time, I played about 60 hours on console before buying on PC.
